data.title: The European Central Bank (ECB) has released a policy targeting unlicensed crypto service providers.
data.description: The European Banking Authority (EBA) issued an opinion on the 12th. Based on the revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), the transition period set forth in the EBA's No-Action Letter of June 2, 2025 (EBA/Op/2025/08) ends on March 2, 2026. The EBA has made recommendations to National Competent Authorities (NCAs) regarding how to proceed after the end of the transition period. The recommendation is to allow Crypto Asset Service Providers (CASPs) to continue trading eligible electronic money tokens (EMTs) for nine months after submitting and awaiting PSD2 authorization applications. The opinion summarizes that it recommends NCAs continue to allow CASPs to offer eligible electronic money tokens (EMTs) after March 2, 2026, even if they do not yet hold a PSD2 license. It also recommends that NCAs require CASPs that do not meet all these conditions to cease offering such EMT services. If necessary, it is recommended that NCAs cooperate with the Crypto Asset Market Regulation (MiCA) and/or other national law enforcement agencies to ensure compliance. This opinion follows the EBA's letter of non-action issued on June 2, 2025, in response to the European Commission's request for clarification regarding the interaction between EMT transaction CASPs eligible for payment services under PSD2 and MiCA. This letter of non-action allows CASPs to continue providing these services and requires a second authorization under PSD2, but not immediately, with a nine-month transition period. Furthermore, the letter recommends that national authorities only consider crypto asset services with EMT as payment services as part of such authorization, thereby reducing the number of CASPs requiring such authorization. The report also recommends that national authorities adopt simplified authorization processes, fully utilizing the information already provided during the MICA authorization process to reduce the administrative burden on CASPs. Since the letter of no action was issued, more than 100 CASPs have informally submitted or submitted applications for authorization as payment service providers (PSPs) to national authorities. With the transition period nearing its end, and given the potential differences in the number of applications across member states, the EBA is issuing this opinion to advise NCAs on how to prioritize their authorization efforts after the transition period ends. Legal Basis: The EBA’s authority to issue this opinion is based on Article 29(1)(a) of Regulation 1093/2010[1], which requires the EBA to actively participate in building a unified regulatory culture and consistent regulatory practices within the Union, and to ensure uniformity of procedures and methods within the Union, including issuing opinions to national authorities.

data.title: UK on-chain government bonds and Thai crypto derivatives: Global sovereigns embracing digital assets
data.description:
The UK government recently appointed HSBC's Orion platform to pilot the issuance of digital gilt-edged bonds (DIGIT), marking the official entry of the sovereign debt market into the blockchain era. Meanwhile, Thailand approved digital assets as derivatives and underlying assets in its capital markets, becoming a new benchmark for crypto trading by Southeast Asian institutions. These two initiatives, though located in the UK and Southeast Asia respectively, together outline the accelerating trend of global sovereign institutions embracing blockchain.

UK: Digital Phnom Penh Bond Pilot

On February 11, the UK Treasury announced that it has designated Orion, HSBC's digital asset platform, as the official pilot issuer for digital government bonds (Phnom Penh bonds). This is another significant development in the sovereign debt sector following the Bank of England's digital pound test in 2024.

Pilot Core: The Orion platform will be responsible for the issuance, settlement, and secondary market circulation of DIGIT (Digital Gilt Instrument Token), using Ethereum or permissioned blockchain technology to achieve T+0 real-time settlement and 24/7 trading. Target Scenarios: Fragmented bondholders can achieve instant splitting and reinvestment through tokenization; institutional investors can embed ESG terms or automated interest payments in the form of smart contracts, improving liquidity and transparency. Regulatory Endorsement: The UK Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has pre-issued guidelines for a regulatory sandbox of tokenized assets to ensure the pilot program complies with the capital adequacy and custody requirements of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2023. HSBC Europe CEO Georges Elhedery stated: "Digital gilt-edged bonds are not just a technological upgrade, but a global paradigm shift in the sovereign debt market." This pilot program directly responds to the UK's Technology Future Strategy, aiming to reduce government financing costs and enhance international attractiveness through blockchain technology. Thailand: Embracing Crypto Derivatives Thousands of miles away, Thailand's Ministry of Finance approved a proposal this Tuesday to formally include digital assets in the list of underlying assets for derivatives and capital markets, making it the first ASEAN jurisdiction to embrace crypto derivatives through national legislation. **Policy Highlights:** Bitcoin, Ethereum, and other mainstream cryptocurrencies and stablecoins can be used as underlying assets or collateral for futures, options, and swap contracts. The Thai Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) will simultaneously issue guidelines on leverage ratios, risk reserves, and investor suitability. **Regional Positioning:** Thailand aims to become an "ASEAN crypto derivatives hub," attracting global institutions to participate in Thai baht stablecoin/BTC/ETH perpetual contract trading through its clearinghouse, directly competing with Singapore and Hong Kong. Infrastructure Support: The Thai Digital Asset Exchange (DAX) will be upgraded to a licensed derivatives platform, introducing an on-chain margin system and cross-chain collateralization, supporting USDT/THB hedging products. Thai Finance Minister Pichai Chunhavajira emphasized that this move aims to "revitalize the traditional derivatives market through crypto assets, while strengthening anti-money laundering and market manipulation prevention." This policy directly echoes Thailand's "Digital Asset Ecosystem Roadmap 2025-2030," with the goal of increasing derivatives trading volume from the current $15 billion per year to the $50 billion level. Extended Impact: The Global Sovereign Blockchain Race Intensifies The actions of the UK and Thailand may seem different, but they point to the same strategic logic: traditional sovereign debt and derivatives markets are limited by T+2 settlement, tiered clearing, and geographical restrictions, while blockchain brings real-time performance and global programmability, directly reducing financing and transaction costs. As the US CLARITY Act and the EU MiCA framework become clearer, sovereign states, fearing marginalization, are eager to attract global asset management giants (such as BlackRock and Fidelity) through "national-level digital asset products." These two developments will trigger a domino effect—Hong Kong/Singapore's counterattack: Following the issuance of the first batch of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong in March, on-chain government bond issuance is expected to proceed simultaneously; the Monetary Authority of Singapore may accelerate the tokenization testing of Project Guardian's derivatives. Pressure from the US and Europe: The UK's DIGIT pilot program will likely spur the US Senate to expedite the GENIUS stablecoin bill; the EU may respond with a combination of a digital euro and sovereign debt tokenization. Emerging Market Opportunities: Brazil, the UAE, India, and other countries may replicate the Thai model, attracting foreign investment through crypto derivatives to achieve limited opening of their capital accounts. From HSBC Orion's on-chain gilt-edged bonds to the Thai SEC's pilot program for BTC perpetual contracts, global sovereign institutions are collectively validating an assumption: blockchain is not only an efficiency tool for private markets, but also the future infrastructure for national credit and monetary policy. When the UK and Thailand launch their "digital sovereign assets," the flow of global asset management funds will be reshaped—the traditional bond and derivatives markets may be facing their final on-chain reshuffle. This article is for reference only and does not constitute any investment advice. The market is risky; invest with caution.

data.title: Understand the economy like you understand an ecosystem
data.description:
The ending of the movie *The Big Short* coincides with the start of the 2008 financial crisis. Aside from a few astute investment geniuses in the film, most people lost everything when the bubble burst. Have you ever pondered this question: Why is mainstream economics so often unable to predict financial crises? Why did the 2008 crash catch all economists off guard? Ethan Buckman was once a biophysicist. When he turned to studying economic systems, he discovered an absurd fact—the core assumption of mainstream economics is "equilibrium." In traditional economic models, experts attempt to bring the system to equilibrium through complex equations, as if the economy were a static machine. But according to the second law of thermodynamics, everything in the universe tends towards chaos, and life exists precisely because of thermal imbalances. This podcast episode, the fourth in the Green Pill series "Ethereum Localism," focuses on alternative economics. Hosts Alex, Max, and Josh invite three deep thinkers to re-examine our fundamental understanding of economics, money, and wealth from the perspectives of biology, anthropology, and community practice. The three guests reach a consensus from different angles: we need to understand the economy like we understand an ecosystem—it's not a machine, but an organism; not static, but fluid; not about possession, but about circulation. Guests: Ethan Buckman. As co-founder of cross-chain giant Cosmos and CEO of Informal Systems, Ethan is not only dedicated to technology development but also deeply involved in software research and education. With a strong background in biophysics, Ethan tends to analyze economic issues from the perspective of complex systems theory and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics. Currently, he is focusing on the development of the Cycles protocol, attempting to solve the debt liquidation problem in the economy through graph theory, and promoting more liquid and sustainable monetary experiments. Julio Linarez is an economic anthropologist from Guatemala, now residing in Berlin. He studied money and value theory at the London School of Economics under the late anthropologist David Graeber and conducted fieldwork on monetary flows in impoverished communities in Bangladesh. Julio is also one of the initiators of the Circles UBI (Universal Basic Income) project, which attempts to self-organize a basic income system without relying on the government. Julio himself is dedicated to exploring anti-colonial financial systems, advocating the abolition of the existing debt-based dollar hegemony, and the establishment of a new economic structure based on community self-governance, direct democracy, and ecological justice. Alana Kanei is a board member of Hours Exchange. Hours Exchange is a community currency project located in Corvallis, Oregon. They emphasize that unlike anonymous transactions involving the US dollar, local currency exchanges create a "relationship economy"—each exchange leaves a trace of interpersonal connection that weaves into a mutual aid network over time. Hours Exchange believes this enduring relationship is more valuable than any single currency or token.

Podcast Time | May 6, 2025

A Leap from Biophysics to Monetary Theory

In our everyday understanding, money and economic laws are often regarded as immutable physical laws, like "universal gravitation."

data.title: Coinbase earnings report: Growth only 9% - Is the best era for exchanges over?
data.description:
Author: David, Deep Tide TechFlow

On February 12th, after the US stock market closed, Coinbase released its Q4 and full-year 2025 financial report.

Full-year revenue was $7.2 billion, a year-on-year increase of 9%. Sounds okay, but last year this growth rate was 115%; Q4 single-quarter revenue was $1.78 billion, a year-on-year decrease of 21.6%, lower than Wall Street's expectation of $1.85 billion. Earnings per share were $0.66, compared to market expectations of $1.05, a difference of 37%.

Market expectations for this financial report have already been reflected in the stock price.

COIN closed at $141 that day, down 68% from its high of $445 in July last year.

After hours, it briefly touched $134, a new low in nearly 52 weeks. However, in its shareholder letter released on the same day, Coinbase was filled with "all-time highs": Year-to-date trading volume doubled, crypto trading market share doubled, USDC holdings hit a record high, and paid subscribers approached 1 million. CEO Brian Armstrong stated in the subsequent earnings call that 2025 would be a "strong year," and Coinbase had secured a significant position. However, on the same day the earnings report was released, Coinbase experienced a technical glitch, preventing some users from trading and transferring funds normally for several hours. The company stated that the issue was under investigation and user assets were safe. But coinciding with the earnings report date was somewhat awkward. A financial report filled with record highs, coupled with a stock price hitting a 52-week low. Coinbase's 2025 presents two contrasting faces. We have analyzed this contradictory performance by combining Coinbase's shareholder letter, earnings call, and publicly available market data. Trading volume doubled, but profits decreased. The contradiction begins with trading volume. In 2025, Coinbase's total transaction volume reached $5.2 trillion, a year-on-year increase of 156%. Its market share in the crypto trading market jumped from 3.2% to 6.4%. Both figures are the highest since the company went public. However, transaction revenue was only $4.1 billion, a year-on-year increase of only 2%. While volume more than doubled, revenue remained almost unchanged. This is because transaction fees were decreasing. In August 2025, Coinbase completed its acquisition of Deribit, a $2.9 billion deal that was the largest merger and acquisition in the history of the crypto industry. Deribit, the world's largest cryptocurrency options exchange, brings in massive derivatives trading volume, but the per-transaction fee for derivatives is far lower than that for spot trading. In other words, the "historic high" in trading volume is somewhat inflated. With a larger market capitalization, the profit per unit is thinner. Looking at Q4 alone, trading revenue was $983 million, the first time it has fallen below $1 billion in six quarters, a year-on-year decline of 36.8%. This occurred against the backdrop of BTC's decline from its all-time high of approximately $126,000 in October of last year, falling back to around $90,000 by the end of Q4. The decline intensified after entering 2026, briefly dipping below $60,000 in early February. The overall market capitalization of the cryptocurrency market shrank by 11% quarter-on-quarter in Q4, with lower volatility and a significant contraction in retail trading activity. According to Zacks data, Q4 spot trading volume for consumers was $59 billion, while institutional trading volume was $237 billion. Institutions supported the volume, but retail investors were leaving the market. Subscription revenue was 5.5 times the peak of the last bull market, but growth is slowing. The good news is in another section. Subscription and service revenue reached $2.8 billion for the year, a 23% year-over-year increase, and is 5.5 times the peak of the 2021 bull market. This revenue now accounts for 41% of net revenue. Breaking it down, stablecoin revenue is the biggest contributor. Q4 stablecoin-related revenue was $364 million, a 61.2% year-over-year increase. The average holding of USDC on the platform reached $17.8 billion, and the total market capitalization of USDC averaged approximately $76 billion in Q4. Coinbase takes a cut from the interest income and circulation revenue of USDC; this revenue is not dependent on user transactions and is more like a "deposit-based interest" model. Coinbase One's paid subscriptions reached nearly 971,000 by the end of 2025, a three-fold increase in three years. Twelve products generated annualized revenue exceeding $100 million, with six exceeding $250 million and two exceeding $1 billion. However, Q4 subscription and service revenue was $727 million, a 3% decrease quarter-over-quarter. Management is also not optimistic about future subscription revenue. During the earnings call, CFO Alesia Haas gave a forecast range of $550 million to $630 million for Q1 2026 subscription and service revenue, a further decrease from Q4. She cited two reasons: the Federal Reserve's 25 basis point rate cuts in October and December of last year, which lowered the interest rate yield on USDC; and the recent continued decline in crypto asset prices, which dragged down staking-related revenue. Armstrong also said in the call that Coinbase is building a "universal exchange" where everything can be traded. Subscription revenue is the new pillar of this story. But under the test of the crypto winter, this pillar is also shaky. Huge losses on paper, but not because the business is bad. According to US Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), Coinbase reported a net loss of $667 million in Q4. This compares to a profit of $1.3 billion in the same period last year. The bulk of the losses isn't in the business itself. Two unrealized investment losses ate up the profit and loss statement. The first is unrealized losses on the crypto portfolio, amounting to $718 million. Coinbase continued to increase its BTC holdings throughout 2025, doubling its total BTC holdings and buying every week. This strategy came at a significant cost during the quarter when BTC plummeted from its peak. The second loss was a strategic investment of $395 million. A large portion of this came from Coinbase's stake in Circle. Circle is the issuer of USDC and one of Coinbase's most important partners. Circle's stock price fell by about 40% in Q4, directly dragging down Coinbase's investment balance sheet. The two losses combined exceed $1.1 billion. However, these are all "unrealized" losses, meaning the assets haven't been sold yet, but have simply been revalued at market price. If BTC recovers, this loss will reverse. If these investment fluctuations are excluded, Coinbase's adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, typically used to measure the profitability of a company's core business) is $566 million, and adjusted net profit is $178 million. By this standard, Coinbase has maintained profitability for 12 consecutive quarters. Herein lies a structural contradiction. Coinbase operates as both an exchange and an asset holder. Its balance sheet is filled with substantial investments in BTC and crypto-related assets. During bull markets, this amplifies profits; a significant portion of the $1.3 billion net profit in 2024 came from investment income. However, in bear markets, the same logic reverses. CFO Haas did not shy away from this issue during the conference call. Her statement was that Coinbase "slightly increased its weekly BTC purchases" in the current price environment. In other words: the price has fallen, but we've bought more. This stance is similar to the logic of Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy), both betting on the long-term value of BTC using their company balance sheets. The difference is that Strategy treats this as its main business, while Coinbase treats it as a side business. But when BTC falls by nearly 50%, even a side business can significantly impact the appearance of financial reports. Finally, the financial report shows that Coinbase had $11.3 billion in cash and equivalents at the end of the year. They're not short of money, but continuously buying BTC and consistently losing money is worrying. What exactly does Coinbase want to become? Cryptocurrency exchanges face an inescapable fate: immense profits in bull markets, halved returns in bear markets. Coinbase, in its four years as a public company, has repeated this pattern in every financial report. In 2025, management attempted to rewrite the script. Armstrong repeatedly mentioned the term "Everything Exchange" during earnings calls. He wanted Coinbase to be more than just a place to buy and sell cryptocurrencies; he wanted it to be a platform where any asset could be traded. In fact, some actions have already been taken. Last Q4, Coinbase opened its prediction market feature to all users, allowing them to bet on real-world events such as sports events and political elections; this February, the platform planned to list nearly 10,000 US stock tickers. Armstrong mentioned in the conference call that trading volumes for prediction markets and gold and silver both broke records in the first quarter of this year. The significance of these new categories is that their trading volumes don't fluctuate with the crypto market. Even if BTC drops by 50%, users can still trade Tesla stock or bet on the Super Bowl on Coinbase. The more diversified the revenue streams, the lower the dependence on crypto cycles. Another big move is Deribit. In terms of derivatives, Coinbase completed its acquisition of Deribit for $2.9 billion in August 2025, capturing approximately 80% of the global crypto options market. Combined with its existing futures and perpetual contract businesses, Coinbase now has a complete derivatives product line. Stablecoins are what Armstrong calls the "second killer app." USDC holdings on the platform hit a record high in Q4, and stablecoin-related revenue grew by over 61% year-over-year. He even proposed a more ambitious vision: AI agents will use stablecoins as the default payment method in the future, with Coinbase's Base Chain serving as the entry point. Stocks, derivatives, prediction markets, stablecoins, AI infrastructure—Coinbase has practically covered every possible sector in 2025, completing 10 acquisitions or talent acquisitions throughout the year. The blueprint for the so-called "universal exchange" has been drawn up, but a bear market is the best time to test the transformation. A detail you might overlook is that around the time of the earnings release, Monness Crespi Hardt downgraded Coinbase's rating from "buy" to "sell," arguing that the market underestimated the duration of the crypto bear market. However, on the other hand, 23 out of 35 analysts still maintained a "buy" rating, with a consensus target price of $326, implying more than double the current share price. Both bulls and bears are betting on the same question: can Coinbase's transformation outpace the cycle? The best era for exchanges may not be over. But the days of doubling revenue and making easy money are unlikely to return. Coinbase itself knows this, which is why it's desperately striving to become a universal exchange. When crypto prices plummet, retail investors leave, and transaction fees fall, are these new sectors truly revenue engines, or merely embellishments to a bull market narrative? The answer may only become clear after the earnings reports are released in two or three quarters.

data.title: What are the bookmakers betting on? Justin Sun personally demonstrates "Sun Studies".
data.description:
Author: Nicky, Foresight News

Welcome back to "Hot Commentary," where we bring you weekly roundups of the most outrageous and mind-blowing moments in Web3 and the tech world!

Tomorrow is Valentine's Day, and the day after is Lunar New Year's Eve! Wishing all lovers a happy ending and everyone a prosperous and lucky Year of the Horse!

Without further ado, let's dive into this week's exciting episodes!

Market downturn, are comedians the first to recoup their losses?

Reddit Hot Post: "What Do We Choose?" The image shows four choices: buy gold or silver, sell crypto at a loss, hold until it goes to zero, or continue buying the dip?

A highly-rated comment hit the nail on the head: "Selling low and buying high? No wonder you're a true buddy in the crypto world!"

Another comment was even more disheartening: someone suggested switching directly to the S&P 500 index, claiming it's the epitome of all bubbles. The commenter replied: "The S&P 500 is the least suitable asset to buy; I won't buy it, thank you."

Faced with the panic of new investors, a veteran with the ID erjo5055 left a classic quote: "Dude, is this all? If you can't even handle this little bit of volatility, you'd better not trade cryptocurrencies."

"I went from a 900% unrealized profit to a 500% loss, do you think I would cry?" A netizen in the comments section posted their real account: currently still with a 500% unrealized profit, and at its peak, the profit exceeded 900%.

" "Do you think I'd cry over a 500% profit?" Some people can't sleep after a 10% pullback, while others think it's just a correction even after their profits are halved. I suggest framing this screenshot and hanging it under every "Should I cut my losses?" post. A long post stands out with its calm: "I've been buying on dips. Heaven or hell, I'm in it. I'm going to weather this bear market like a 'brainless bull.' If the next bull market really comes, my position will be very comfortable. The simplest profit model is to buy in a bear market and sell in a bull market! And those who lose money are always the ones who wait until the bull market to start buying." This passage is worth memorizing in its entirety. "Don't build positions in a bear market, or you'll waste your time in a bull market." Everyone understands this principle, but those who can't put it into practice are usually eliminated in the previous bear markets. "What were we celebrating two years ago?" Someone unearthed an old screenshot from February 2024: Bitcoin price at $40,420, the whole internet was jubilant! "Never forget, two years ago we were popping champagne at this price." $40,000 was once the starting point of a bull market, now it's the support level of a bear market. The cruelest humor in the crypto world is called "cyclicality."

Two crypto heavyweights engaged in a war of words this week.

Former Multicoin Capital co-founder Kyle Samani launched an attack on Hyperliquid, using extremely harsh language: "The founder fled his home country, openly supports crime, has closed-source code, requires permissions—this project has all the wrong answers in the crypto world."

BitMEX co-founder Arthur Hayes quickly responded with a counterattack.

Instead of refuting each point, he presented a bet: "Since you say HYPE is no good, let's make a bet. From February 10th to July 31st, HYPE will outperform any 'junk coin' you choose with a market capitalization exceeding $1 billion (in USD). The loser will donate $100,000 to a charity designated by the winner." As of press time, Kyle's account remains silent, having not yet accepted the challenge.

Mr. Samani, what do you call Hyperliquid? Hyper liquid? Super liquid?

... Samani did not reply; perhaps he has gone offline? The betting standard might be too low; even the SEC could easily meet it. Perhaps Polymarket is better suited to solve this problem, since "predicting" events is better suited to meet real-world needs in "prediction markets."

Some netizens commented, "Hayes, can you please stop with the 'fraud'? Are you trying to trick me into buying HYPE this time?"

A netizen commented: Mr. Hayes, after BitMEX listed HYPE, the price dropped from $30 to $9. Is this a new "promotional tactic"?

Game theory suggests that the house always wins, but here both sides are house players. Who will ultimately lose?

At the Hong Kong Consensus Conference, Justin Sun took a pen

At this week's Hong Kong Consensus Conference, Justin Sun arrived as scheduled and held a Tron event before the conference.

... At the event, a blogger enthusiastically handed over a notebook for Sun Yuchen to sign. After signing, did Sun Yuchen just take the pen away? After the video was posted, the blogger commented: "Sun Yuchen finally stopped being stingy today, he actually gave me a T-shirt! And I even got an autograph, this is a huge win!" However, netizens commented: "You think you got a T-shirt and an autograph, but actually Sun Yuchen got your pen and hand cream." You might have gotten a great deal, but Sun Yuchen never loses! A netizen who received a T-shirt from Brother Sun as a gift washed it with a coat they bought for over 10,000 yuan. The T-shirt bled color, and the coat was stained. They can't help but light a cigarette whenever they think about it. You think you're taking a photo with Brother Sun, but actually Brother Sun has dozens of ways to cut you in his mind. A netizen shared: "I bought Brother Sun's book, *This World Is Both Cruel and Gentle*, finished it in two days, returned it within seven days with a refund, and even used shipping insurance. This is the first time I've gotten a 'good deal' from Brother Sun." Brother Sun responded: "Graduated from Sun Studies?" This matter, well, it's not a big deal, but it's really funny. Even in the crypto bear market, Brother Sun maintains his excellent tradition of accumulating every last bit of profit. A person with vision never lets any pen go to waste. Next time you see Brother Sun, I suggest you pull your hand back after handing him your notebook, just in case your hand cream gets stolen! Tomorrow is Valentine's Day, and the day after is Lunar New Year's Eve! May the new year bring you the calm mindset of a comedian, the wealth of a banker, and the steady hand of Brother Sun's pen. Happy Year of the Horse! See you after the New Year! (This content is based on public information and online discussions, presented in a lighthearted manner, for reference only.)

data.title: Lost in Hong Kong
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.txt; Source: BlockBeats

A city that uses yesterday's rules to embrace tomorrow's innovation is destined to lose its way today.

Today's Hong Kong is shrouded in a tremendous sense of division. Or rather, it's more like two Hong Kongs in parallel universes, folded within the same city.

One ​​Hong Kong, within the skyscrapers of Central.

In 2025, Hong Kong's economy is expected to perform strongly, with real GDP growth projected to reach 3.2% for the year. The Hang Seng Index is expected to rise 27.8%, marking its best annual performance since 2017. Merchandise exports are expected to reach a record high, with December's export value reaching HK$512.8 billion, a year-on-year increase of 26.1%.

Net capital inflows remain robust, and private wealth management assets have surpassed the HK$10 trillion mark. Here, the bubbling of champagne never ceases; prosperity is the sole theme. Another Hong Kong exists: in the shared office spaces of Cyberport, on the streets of Sham Shui Po, and at the crowded Lok Ma Chau border crossing. Entrepreneurs struggle to survive amidst high costs; shop closures are frequent in sectors like retail; the unemployment rate is projected to rise gradually by 2025; and more and more Hong Kong residents are abandoning their former "shopping paradise" status, heading north to Shenzhen and Guangzhou to spend their money. Here, a sense of loss and confusion permeates the air. On one hand, financial data is booming; on the other, the public feels like they are walking on thin ice. This extreme contradiction is a true reflection of Hong Kong's current situation. Hong Kong is resting on its laurels, trying to cope with today's new changes using yesterday's experience, and the result is obvious. Two Crises and Muscle Memory Hong Kong's current rules are actually the result of two painful lessons: the 1997 Asian financial crisis and the 2008 global financial tsunami. These two crises thoroughly hurt Hong Kong and instilled fear in it. It has even developed muscle memory; its first reaction to any risk is to withdraw its hand. In 1997, Hong Kong had just returned to China, and international speculators, led by George Soros' Quantum Fund, set their sights on this lucrative opportunity. They shorted the Hong Kong dollar with one hand and shorted Hong Kong stocks with the other, attempting to directly collapse Hong Kong's exchange rate system. A fierce battle, known as the "Hong Kong Financial Defense War," ensued. Donald Tsang, then Financial Secretary of Hong Kong, later recalled that it was a period that was almost suffocating. Soros and his ilk, through short selling and lending, orchestrated a complex scheme in the stock and currency markets. They first dumped large amounts of Hong Kong dollars in the currency market, forcing the Hong Kong Monetary Authority to raise interest rates to maintain exchange rate stability. High interest rates inevitably led to a stock market decline, and subsequently, they took advantage of the situation to close out their massive short positions. Thus, the operation was complete, and the stock and currency markets became their double ATMs. Faced with this attack, the Hong Kong government initially retreated. However, on August 14, 1998, the Hong Kong government decided to use its foreign exchange reserves to directly intervene in the market. Over the next ten trading days, the Hong Kong government invested a total of HK$118 billion (approximately US$15 billion), confronting the international speculators head-on. On August 28th, the settlement day for Hang Seng Index futures, the trading volume of the Hang Seng Index reached a record high of HK$79 billion. The Hong Kong government held firm at 7829 points, ultimately forcing Soros and his ilk to settle at a high level and suffer a crushing defeat. This victory, though brutal, at least preserved the linked exchange rate, Hong Kong's financial lifeline. However, it left behind lingering problems, leading regulators to establish an ironclad rule: stability is paramount. Any factor that could potentially threaten financial stability must be subject to the most rigorous scrutiny. This is Hong Kong's first layer of muscle memory. If the 1997 crisis was merely an external shock, then the 2008 crisis was a fire in its own backyard. Although the fire originated in the United States, what ultimately burned away was the trust of Hong Kong citizens in the elites of Central. That year, Lehman Brothers collapsed across the ocean, and its aftershocks swept through Hong Kong like a tsunami. More than 40,000 Hong Kong citizens, the vast majority of whom were elderly people living on pensions, saw their mini-bonds linked to Lehman Brothers become worthless overnight. These mini-bonds were packaged as low-risk, high-yield financial products and sold through banks to ordinary people with the least risk tolerance. This event exposed regulatory loopholes and sales misrepresentation within Hong Kong's financial system, severely shaking public trust in financial institutions. This event directly spurred stricter investor protection regulations and more complex financial product sales processes in Hong Kong. It led regulators to adopt an almost obsessive-compulsive cautious attitude towards any financial innovation that might trigger systemic risks, especially those that could harm the interests of ordinary investors. This is Hong Kong's second layer of "muscle memory." It was only after experiencing these two crises that Hong Kong's financial regulatory system embarked on a path that extremely emphasizes stability and security. This path dependence has helped Hong Kong successfully withstand external shocks time and again over the past two decades, but it has also made it incompatible with, and even ill-suited to, a completely new fintech revolution characterized by disruption and decentralization. So, what kind of fractured economic reality has this muscle memory, rooted in historical trauma, given rise to in Hong Kong today? A Divided Hong Kong: Whose Prosperity? Whose Loss? The wounds of history have ultimately carved three deep chasms into Hong Kong's landscape. Today, Hong Kong is experiencing a comprehensive economic fragmentation. The first chasm is between finance and the real economy. While global capital brokers and investment bankers are once again focusing their attention on Hong Kong, celebrating its return to the top of the global IPO fundraising list, Hong Kong's real economy is experiencing a prolonged winter. According to data from the Hong Kong Official Receiver's Office, 589 company winding-up petitions were filed in 2024, a record high since the SARS outbreak in 2003. Within a year, over 500 shops quietly closed down, including many long-established local brands like China Resources Pharmacy, Tai Cheong Foods, and Sincere Department Store, which had been a part of the lives of generations of Hong Kong people. The once-desirable locations in Causeway Bay and Tsim Sha Tsui, where shops were in high demand, are now replaced by rows of closed shutters and "For Rent" signs. The downturn in the real economy is directly reflected in the job market. Hong Kong's overall unemployment rate is projected to remain around 3% for most of 2025, with the retail, accommodation, and food and beverage service sectors experiencing significantly higher rates. The unemployment rate among young people aged 20 to 29 remains particularly high. On one hand, job postings in the financial industry are booming, and traders' bonuses are hitting new highs; on the other hand, layoffs in the retail sector continue unabated, leaving ordinary citizens' jobs in jeopardy. Prosperity has never been so concentrated; despair has never been so widespread. The second chasm is between the elite and the general public. If the gap between finance and the real economy depicts the stark contrast in industrial fortunes, then the gap between the elite and the general public reveals the alienation of hearts and minds. This alienation is most directly reflected in the flow of money and people. On the one hand, global billionaires and mainland elites are voting with their money, flocking to Hong Kong. In 2024, Hong Kong's asset and wealth management business recorded a net inflow of HK$705 billion, a record high. Mainland buyers' transaction volume and value in Hong Kong's property market surged nearly tenfold, purchasing HK$138 billion worth of residential properties within a year. The booming sales of luxury homes worth over HK$100 million seemed completely unaffected by economic cycles. On the other hand, ordinary Hong Kong citizens voted with their feet, flocking to the mainland. In 2024, the total number of Hong Kong residents making trips to the mainland reached 77 million, spending nearly HK$55.7 billion there. From meals and milk tea to dental care and skincare, Shenzhen and Zhuhai became the top weekend destinations for Hong Kong residents. A deeper level of population mobility is reflected in cross-border marriages, education, and elder care. According to data from the Hong Kong Census and Statistics Department, the proportion of Hong Kong women marrying mainland men surged from 6.1% in 1991 to 40% in 2024; more than 30,000 cross-border students travel between Shenzhen and Hong Kong daily; and nearly 100,000 Hong Kong seniors choose to retire in Guangdong, enjoying lower prices and more spacious living environments. While a city's elites discuss the globalization of asset allocation, its citizens are contemplating which restaurant in Shenzhen is the cheapest and most affordable for their next meal. On one hand, there is the fading glory of a financial empire; on the other, a stagnant undercurrent. The third chasm is between assets and innovation. Hong Kong has never lacked money, but it seems that the money hasn't flowed to where it's most needed. Hong Kong's R&D intensity, or R&D expenditure as a percentage of GDP, has hovered around 1.13% for many years. This figure is less than half that of Singapore, and even only a quarter of that of South Korea. Even more worrying is that Shenzhen, just across the river, has already crossed the 5% threshold for R&D intensity. Although the number of startups in Hong Kong reached 4,694 in 2024, a year-on-year increase of 10%, the average size is only 3.8 people, showing a predicament of numerous small startups but few large ones. Capital prefers to chase assets with high certainty, such as real estate and stocks, rather than high-risk, long-term technological innovation. High housing prices and rental costs have also severely squeezed the survival space of startups. In Hong Kong, the biggest headache for a young entrepreneur is often not finding a good direction, but being unable to pay next month's office rent. The gap between finance and the real economy, the gap between elites and the general public, and the gap between assets and innovation together create a bizarre and surreal economic landscape in Hong Kong. It's like Mordor in the Marvel Universe: its head (finance) is exceptionally developed, while its body and limbs (the real economy and innovation) are gradually shrinking. So, in such a fertile ground, how will the seed of financial innovation take root and sprout? Will it transform this soil, or will it be transformed by it? There are no winners in financial innovation. The answer is that it has been transformed by this soil. From the very beginning, Hong Kong's fintech innovation has been a top-down, strictly controlled reform movement. Here, you cannot both tame the flames and expect them to illuminate the darkness. The first battlefield of this reform movement was payment. Once upon a time, a small Octopus card was an innovation that Hong Kong people were proud of. But when the mobile payment wave swept the globe, Octopus responded slowly, giving mainland payment giants and local innovators enormous room for imagination. However, what ultimately unified the battlefield was neither WeChat Pay or Alipay, nor any ambitious startup, but two products with strong "official" and "establishment" characteristics: HSBC's PayMe and the Faster Payment System (FPS) led by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority. PayMe, born with a silver spoon in its mouth, backed by HSBC, Hong Kong's largest note-issuing bank, possessed a huge existing customer base and unparalleled brand trust. The Faster Payment System (FPS) is an interbank payment infrastructure built directly by regulatory agencies. Their victory is less a victory of products and more a victory of "order." This so-called payment war was never a fair contest from the start. Traditional financial giants and regulators, by proactively launching improved products, successfully kept other innovators out and consolidated their positions. If innovation in the payment sector was initially fostered, then virtual banks were expected to act as "catfish." In 2019, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority issued eight virtual bank licenses in one go, hoping to introduce these "catfish" to stir up the stagnant traditional banking industry. However, five years later, the eight virtual banks have burned through more than HK$10 billion but have only gained less than 0.3% of the market share.

Until the first half of 2025, ZA Bank, WeBank, and Invesco Great Wall finally recorded profits for the first time, but the eight banks still suffered a combined loss of HK$610 million. The vast majority of Hong Kong citizens still use traditional banks as their primary accounts, with virtual banks more like an e-wallet occasionally used for transfers or "coupon hunting."

They haven't disrupted anything; instead, they've fallen into a survival crisis. The few virtual banks that have achieved profitability haven't relied on disruptive innovation in products or services, but rather on finding their niche by exploiting gray areas that traditional banks are unwilling to touch, such as providing account services to Web3 companies.

This is less a victory for the "catfish" and more a compromise of being co-opted. They ultimately didn't become challengers, but rather patches to the existing financial system. An even more interesting phenomenon occurred in the wealth technology and insurtech sectors. These two sectors were listed by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority as the fintech tracks with the greatest growth potential and the most worthy of support, because they perfectly align with Hong Kong's core interests: consolidating its position as a global asset management center. Wealth technology makes asset management more convenient for high-net-worth clients, while insurtech makes the sale of insurance products more efficient. They are safe innovations serving the existing order, tools that add icing on the cake. Thus, we see a "selective prosperity" in Hong Kong's fintech sector. On one hand, there's the booming scene of assets under management exceeding HK$35 trillion; on the other hand, virtual banks are struggling with a mere 0.3% market share. Fintech serving the wealthy has enjoyed smooth sailing, while those aiming to serve the masses and reshape the landscape have encountered obstacles at every turn. From payments and banking to wealth management, Hong Kong's fintech innovation exhibits a clear pattern: embracing reform while resisting innovation. This has ultimately evolved into a war with no winners. While traditional giants have preserved their competitive advantages, they may have consequently lost the drive for more thorough and future-oriented innovation; and those passionate innovators, after paying a heavy price, have ultimately failed to truly change the rules of the game in this market. If Hong Kong has chosen such a conservative path of appeasement even in relatively mature fintech sectors like payments and banking, then what choice will it make when faced with Web3, a truly wild, unpredictable revolution driven by decentralization? The Hong Kong-Style Awkwardness of Web3 If Hong Kong's approach to fintech is one of appeasement, then its approach to Web3 is a different kind of choice: embracing its form while rejecting its essence. At the end of 2022, Hong Kong announced its ambition to become a global virtual asset center. Policy pronouncements poured in, from retail investor access to stablecoin regulatory sandboxes, Hong Kong adopted a posture of embracing the future. However, more than two years later, what we have received is a typical Hong Kong-style awkwardness. In April 2024, when Hong Kong launched Asia's first batch of virtual asset spot ETFs before the US, the entire market was abuzz. However, the honeymoon period was as short-lived as a summer downpour. By the end of 2025, the total assets under management of Hong Kong's six virtual asset ETFs amounted to only US$529 million, more than 80 times less than the over US$45.7 billion of similar products in the US. The same ETFs, the same assets, but the difference is stark; Hong Kong's market is more like the north. A deeper predicament lies beneath the tight constraints of compliance. Hong Kong has established one of the world's most stringent regulatory standards for virtual asset trading platforms. While this has prevented a collapse similar to FTX, it has also brought extremely high compliance costs. According to industry insiders, a licensed virtual asset trading platform in Hong Kong has monthly operating costs of up to US$10 million, with 30% to 40% allocated to compliance, legal affairs, and auditing. While this might be manageable for giants, it represents an insurmountable obstacle for the vast majority of small and medium-sized startups. The JPEX incident in 2023 further amplified this predicament. This unlicensed trading platform, through aggressive online and offline marketing, lured a large number of ordinary citizens with the promise of "zero risk and high returns," ultimately collapsing with a total amount involved reaching HK$1.6 billion. The negative impact of the JPEX incident lies in its societal effect of creating a "bad money drives out good" scenario, fostering widespread distrust of the Web3 industry among the general public and reinforcing regulatory bodies' determination to maintain strict control. This creates a paradoxical cycle: the pursuit of absolute security increases regulatory costs; higher costs make it harder for licensed institutions to compete with unregulated "wild platforms"; and the collapse of these "wild platforms" further reinforces the necessity of absolute security. Hong Kong attempted to build a rock-solid safe haven for Web3, only to find that once the house was built, innovators either chose to start from scratch outside or suffocated by the cumbersome rules inside. Thus, Hong Kong's true attitude towards Web3 is now crystal clear. It welcomes cryptocurrencies as an alternative asset integrated into the existing financial system—a financial product that can be valued, traded, and managed. But it rejects, or rather fears, cryptocurrencies as a tool of innovation, the kind of Web3 whose core spirit is decentralization, censorship resistance, and the disruption of intermediaries. The former could add a new segment to Hong Kong's asset management landscape, while the latter could shake the very foundations of the entire landscape. This is the most fundamental conflict between "yesterday's rules" and "tomorrow's innovation." Hong Kong is using the logic of managing stocks, bonds, and real estate to manage a new species. As a result, it has rejected innovation itself, leaving only a policy solo performance that no one applauds. Looking back at Hong Kong's fintech journey is like observing the difficult turn of a giant ship. This behemoth named Hong Kong has undoubtedly been one of the most successful ships in the world over the past half-century. However, financial innovation brought not a storm, but a drop in sea level and the rise of new continents. It exposed countless narrow, winding, and reef-strewn new channels in the once unfathomable ocean. In these new channels, the behemoth's advantages became its most fatal disadvantages. It was too large to turn in narrow channels; it was too heavy to navigate in shallow waters; its navigation system completely failed in the new hydrological environment. There is a concept in economics called the "curse of the victors." It refers to how past great success can create a strong path dependency and mindset, making it impossible to adapt to new paradigms, ultimately leading to being devoured by one's past success. This is perhaps the most accurate summary of Hong Kong's current predicament. Hong Kong's disorientation lies not in what it did wrong, but in repeating the right things it did in the past at the wrong time. It attempted to embrace a moveable feast by building fortresses; it tried to embrace a revolution aimed at complete overhaul by reforming the old system. Today, this giant ship is anchored in the middle of Victoria Harbour, its engines still roaring, but the captain and crew are lost in deep confusion. On the distant horizon, countless nimble speedboats are swiftly navigating new routes, leaving it far behind. History has never granted any city permanent immunity. When yesterday's glory becomes today's shackles, only the courage to break free of those shackles can win tomorrow.

data.title: Shenzhen financial regulators set red lines for gold trading.
data.description: On February 13th, Beijing time, the Shenzhen Municipal Financial Regulatory Bureau issued a notice regarding prohibited activities for enterprises, individuals, financial institutions, and non-bank payment institutions engaged in gold business. Regarding enterprises, the notice states that they are prohibited from engaging in illegal gold trading activities such as pre-sale gold trading, leveraged trading, and deferred trading. This includes activities such as allowing customers to pay a certain amount of margin to lock in the price of a certain weight of gold under the guise of gold recycling and pre-sale gold trading through WeChat groups, mini-programs, apps, websites, or other internet platforms, with settlement based on price differences as the gold price fluctuates, allowing for liquidation without actual physical gold delivery. Secondly, they are prohibited from engaging in illegal fundraising activities under the guise of gold custody, leasing, or repurchase, promising customers fixed returns with "principal and interest guaranteed"; and from engaging in illegal gold entrusted investment activities, such as inducing consumers to purchase physical gold and then engaging in illegal financial activities like gold entrusted investment without taking delivery of the physical gold. Third, it is prohibited to use internet live streaming or other channels to illegally promote or conduct gold product sales; it is prohibited to conduct illegal gold trading activities through various software, apps, and mini-programs; it is prohibited to develop or sell illegal gold trading software, apps, or mini-programs; and it is prohibited to provide information services and technical support for illegal gold trading software, apps, or mini-programs. Regarding individuals, the announcement advises that qualified individual investors can participate in gold exchange-traded funds (i.e., gold ETFs) and gold futures trading through legitimate channels. Individuals can purchase physical gold through legitimate gold shops or banks, and purchase gold accumulation products or gold-based wealth management products from banks. Sun Lin, director of the Shenzhen Financial Crime Law Professional Committee, pointed out that unlike previous industry risk warnings that informed consumers and investors of "potential risks that must be borne by themselves," the current announcement explicitly uses terms such as "illegal" and "illegal fundraising activities." He believes this signifies that the regulatory attitude has been upgraded from "warning" to "alarm." In response, Leng Yongda, a lawyer at Beijing Zhonglun Wende (Shenzhen) Law Firm, stated that recently, the Shuibei International Jewelry Exchange Center (hereinafter referred to as "Shuibei") has experienced several cases of insolvency due to illegal gold trading, causing huge losses to a large number of consumers. Therefore, Shenzhen is expected to launch a crackdown on companies engaged in illegal gold trading and strictly investigate such companies that do not have the qualifications to operate gold trading. Around January 20, 2026, the Shenzhen Shuibei gold pre-order platform "Jiewo Rui" experienced payment difficulties. Public information shows that "Jiewo Rui" is controlled by Shenzhen Jiewo Rui Jewelry Co., Ltd., a gold raw material trader in Shenzhen Shuibei. In recent years, its business has gradually shifted to precious metal retail and opened gold pre-order trading to retail investors. At the end of January, the "Yundian Dang" platform also experienced payment abnormalities. It is understood that this platform is similar to the "Jieworui" model. Users can buy and sell gold on "Yundiandang," locking in the gold price at the time of order placement. Once the platform actually receives the physical gold, settlement must be completed at the locked price regardless of price fluctuations. If the user fails to deliver the gold within the stipulated period, the platform will deduct the corresponding amount of deposit. On January 28, the Luohu District Working Group in Shenzhen issued a notice stating that it had recently discovered abnormal operations at Shenzhen "Jieworui," a company within its jurisdiction. Luohu District attached great importance to this and immediately formed a working group to intervene and urge the company to fulfill its main responsibilities. On January 31, the Luohu District Working Group issued another notice stating that, under the supervision of the working group, "Jieworui" had disposed of assets and raised funds, and had initiated repayment; it had opened online signing and repayment channels and established offline contact points; and it had commissioned a third-party professional institution to audit the company's operations. Preliminary results showed that "the amount circulating online was significantly exaggerated." Currently, many consumers who participated in the transactions have reported that they have not received the payments from "Jiewo Rui". On September 26, 2025, the Shenzhen Gold and Jewelry Industry Association issued a proposal to its colleagues in the Shuibei gold and jewelry industry, stating that in recent years, the business environment of the Shuibei market has been continuously optimized, and the industry's reputation has been constantly improved. However, against the backdrop of current high and volatile gold prices, some merchants, under the guise of physical gold trading, are illegally conducting "gold investment" businesses with a futures-like nature. Such behavior harbors significant risks such as high-leverage gambling and broken capital chains, which not only seriously disrupt market order but also directly damage the overall image of "Shuibei". The judicial authorities have filed cases to investigate and punish the relevant illegal activities, and those responsible will be held accountable according to law. This is a profound lesson and a constant warning. Sun Lin stated that this model that has emerged in Shuibei in recent years can be defined as the unbridled growth of "Internet + traditional gold shops" in a regulatory vacuum. The core risk lies in "financialization without physical backing." He stated that this is not an isolated case, but rather an industry-wide phenomenon occurring in the regulatory blind spot of "social finance." As regulations tighten, platforms lacking physical backing and exploiting information asymmetry to "hunt" investors are collapsing in droves. Sun Lin explained that this model involves the risks of speculative buying and selling and betting. Platforms often do not hold equivalent amounts of physical gold reserves, but instead allow users to "pre-order" and buy/sell on a mini-program. This essentially exhibits characteristics of "over-the-counter options" or "illegal futures trading." When gold prices fluctuate significantly, if the platform does not conduct physical hedging, it will incur huge losses leading to margin calls. Furthermore, he stated that this model often involves illegal fund pools. User funds are not held in custody by banks or other third-party institutions, but instead go directly into the platform's account or multiple individual accounts, making them extremely vulnerable to misappropriation, illegal possession, or absconding with the funds. Zhang Jingshu, a lawyer at Beijing Zhongyin (Shenzhen) Law Firm, pointed out that simple physical heavy metal trading, due to the physical delivery involved and with gold trading as the main business, constitutes a legal gold pre-pricing model, which is relatively popular in Shuibei. This model subsequently evolved into gambling transactions and illegal futures activities, suspected of being illegal and criminal. The "Futures and Derivatives Law of the People's Republic of China" stipulates that without the approval of the futures regulatory authority under the State Council, no unit or individual may establish or indirectly establish a futures company, operate or indirectly operate futures brokerage business or futures trading consulting business, nor may they use "futures," "options," or other names that may cause confusion or misleading for business purposes. The law stipulates that those who illegally establish futures companies or engage in related futures business without approval shall be banned, their illegal gains confiscated, and be fined between one and ten times the amount of their illegal gains; if there are no illegal gains or the illegal gains are less than one million yuan, a fine of between one million and ten million yuan shall be imposed. The directly responsible supervisors and other directly responsible personnel will be given a warning and fined between 200,000 and 2 million yuan. Previously, gold prices had risen for more than three consecutive years. At the beginning of 2026, gold experienced a rollercoaster ride. Spot gold broke through several important levels from around $4,320 per ounce, reaching a peak of $5,598 per ounce. Its cumulative increase once reached 29%. In the early morning of January 31, spot gold's intraday drop widened to a maximum of 12%, hitting a low of $4,682 per ounce, experiencing the most dramatic single-day decline in more than 40 years. The Shenzhen Municipal Financial Regulatory Bureau's announcement also states that it is prohibited to impersonate a "Shanghai Gold Exchange member" or to engage in false or misleading commercial advertising regarding product sales, thereby deceiving or misleading consumers or other businesses. The use of absolute terms such as "gold prices will skyrocket" or "make big money buying gold" is strictly prohibited, as is exaggerating product value or investment returns. Furthermore, it is forbidden to use non-precious metals to impersonate pure gold or other precious materials, and it is strictly prohibited to adulterate or falsify products, sell inferior goods as superior ones, or add extra charges beyond the listed price or collect fees not clearly indicated.

data.title: Russian Officials Confirm No Plans to Block Google Despite YouTube and WhatsApp Bans: Here’s Why
data.description:
Moscow holds back on total Google ban to protect Android users

Russian authorities are walking a tightrope between digital sovereignty and the practical reality of their citizens' pockets.

While the video-sharing giant YouTube and messaging services like WhatsApp have faced the full weight of local restrictions, the Kremlin is stopping short of a complete blackout on Google’s primary search engine.

The hesitation isn’t born of a sudden change of heart toward Western tech, but rather a calculated move to prevent millions of Russian smartphones from becoming expensive paperweights.

Why is Russia avoiding a total block on Google

The risk of a total ban lies in the palm of the average citizen's hand.

Anton Gorelkin, the first deputy chairman of the State Duma’s Committee on Information Policy, clarified that "as for big statements about bans, there are in fact no such plans. I specifically asked the regulators about it."

❗️A possible Google ban in Russia would entail a whole range of negative effects, but such plans do not currently exist.

This was stated by Anton Gorelkin, First Deputy Chairman of the State Duma Committee on Information Policy.

The deputy made this comment in response to… pic.twitter.com/s338TScMq1

— Moscow calling (@moscow__calling) February 12, 2026

The primary concern is the Android operating system, which currently powers 60% of smartphones across the country.

Severing ties with Google could essentially break the core functionality of these devices, leading to a domestic backlash that Moscow is not yet ready to manage.

Gorelkin noted that "a ban would clearly entail an entire set of negative consequences," suggesting that any departure from Google must be a slow, legislative transition toward domestic alternatives rather than an overnight cutoff.

Can technical barriers stop a digital iron curtain

While the political will to restrict foreign platforms is high, the physical infrastructure of the Russian internet faces its own limits.

Experts cited by the business portal RBC suggest that the national firewall may not currently have the sheer capacity to block every major Western platform simultaneously without causing widespread technical instability.

Despite this, some officials believe the capability exists if the situation demands it.

Andrey Svintsov, another senior member of the Duma’s information committee, told Govorit Moskva radio that "in my opinion, it is quite technically feasible. I’m not sure it’s necessary right now."

For now, the strategy remains selective, targeting specific apps while keeping the foundational search and mobile services intact.

Will legal pressure force Google to pay fines

Beyond technical logistics, there is a financial game of chicken at play.

Russian courts have imposed massive fines on Google following lawsuits from local firms, and officials are wary that a total ban would remove any remaining incentive for the company to settle.

🇷🇺 Google could be blocked in Russia, according to statements in the State Duma. Deputy Svintsov said the move is technically feasible.

He suggested that #Roskomnadzor has not yet restricted the browser or related services because it expects the company to pay Russia a debt of… pic.twitter.com/hxUeQvxqRU

— Russian Market (@runews) February 12, 2026

Lawmakers are holding out hope that keeping the door slightly ajar might lead to a compromise.

Gorelkin explained that "the story is not over yet as hearings continue in foreign courts and various legal mechanisms are being employed."

He suggested that even if the fines aren't paid in full, keeping Google active allows the parties to potentially "reach an agreement on reasonable terms."

How is YouTube facing the brunt of the crackdown

The leniency shown to Google’s search engine does not extend to its sister platform, YouTube.

The regulator Roskomnadzor has already taken the step of removing the site’s domain from its DNS servers, effectively blocking access for many users after a period of heavy throttling.

This follows the designation of Meta as an "extremist" organisation, which led to the blocking of WhatsApp, Facebook, and Instagram.

Russia blocked WhatsApp, YouTube, Facebook, Instagram, and ~13 other services. Technically domains were removed from the National DNS (NSDI) a state-controlled domain name system that all Russian ISPs are required to use. This makes internet services unresolvable, effectively… pic.twitter.com/OgawBSl9s3

— Lukasz Olejnik (@lukOlejnik) February 12, 2026

Even the popular messenger Telegram has seen its voice call services limited.

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov has been firm on the requirements for these platforms to return to full service, stating they must start "complying with Moscow’s terms" while encouraging users to migrate to the state-approved messenger, Max.

🚨🇷🇺 BREAKING: RUSSIA has released their MAX messenger app after RESTRICTING the use of foreign-owned Telegram & WhatsApp in their country pic.twitter.com/SeXAOzsksu

— Legitimate Targets (@LegitTargets) September 13, 2025

data.title: Crypto Used to Fund Human Trafficking Explodes 85% in 2025 With Hundreds of Millions at Risk
data.description:
Dark Side Of Decentralisation Revealed

The promise of borderless financial freedom has taken a grim turn as organised crime syndicates increasingly adopt digital assets to power human exploitation.

According to the latest crime report from analytics firm Chainalysis, cryptocurrency flows linked to suspected human trafficking networks skyrocketed by 85% in 2025.

In the next chapter of our 2026 Crypto Crime Report, we reveal that cryptocurrency flows to suspected human trafficking services grew by 85% between 2024 and 2025. See our complete analysis: https://t.co/YU6U0uMTI4

— Chainalysis (@chainalysis) February 12, 2026

These operations, primarily concentrated in Southeast Asia, are now moving hundreds of millions of dollars annually, bypassing the traditional banking safeguards that would normally flag such suspicious activity.

Are Stablecoins The New Currency For Crime?

While Bitcoin often dominates the headlines, the machinery of modern slavery runs on stablecoins like Tether and USDC.

Criminal networks favour these assets because they offer the stability of the US dollar with the frictionless speed of the blockchain.

For international escort services, this technology has become the standard payment rail, with nearly half of all transactions exceeding $10,000.

Incredible report from @chainalysis that shows #crypto#payment flows to suspected human traffickers grew 85% in 2025, dominated by #stablecoin & with almost 50% >$10k. Sobering analysis for unregulated digital asset movement. cc @UnseenUK@Anti_Slavery. https://t.co/9CfRh0eQCXpic.twitter.com/PXxZVRUwvc

— Rob Fernandes (@rob2775) February 13, 2026

Unlike the individual operations of the past, these figures point to industrial-scale coordination.

These services often integrate with sophisticated Chinese-language money laundering systems and guarantee platforms such as Xinbi and Tudou.

These digital middlemen are notoriously resilient; whenever a channel is moderated or shut down, they rebuild their infrastructure within hours, ensuring the money continues to flow.

Can Encryption Protect Both Victims And Villains?

The privacy features of messaging apps like Telegram, which serve as a haven for free speech, have also been weaponised.

Prostitution networks show a distinct pattern where 62% of transactions fall between $1,000 and $10,000, frequently linking back to the same encrypted guarantee services.

International escort services on Telegram are no longer small-time; they are organised enterprises.

Source: Chainalysis

Tom McLouth from Chainalysis explains that “borderless, low-fee payments have created the opportunity for human trafficking to scale faster.”

This digital efficiency allows traffickers to manage exploitation across borders with a level of anonymity that cash simply cannot provide.

How Do Scam Compounds Fuel Forced Labour?

One of the most disturbing developments is the rise of scam compounds in Myanmar, Cambodia, and Laos.

These facilities are essentially modern-day slave colonies where victims are forced to conduct cryptocurrency fraud.

The recruitment process itself has been commodified on Telegram, with recruitment bounties for trafficked workers ranging from $8,888 to $22,000 per person.

Source: Chainalysis

Victims are often lured from South Asia and Africa with the promise of legitimate tech jobs, only to be held captive and forced to staff fake trading platforms or romance scams.

Groups like Fully Light, previously flagged by the UN Office on Drugs and Crime, orchestrate these compounds, which generate tens of billions of dollars in fraudulent revenue every year.

Telegram screenshot showing the recruitment channel’s administrators, including an account linked to Fully Light marked as an “admin.” (Source: Chainalysis)

Is The Blockchain A Tool For Justice?

Despite the surge in illicit activity, the immutable nature of the blockchain is proving to be a double-edged sword for criminals.

Because every transaction is recorded on a public ledger, investigators can trace the movement of funds with a level of precision impossible in the world of physical cash.

Authorities scored significant wins in 2025, including DOJ charges against the Prince Group for scam compound operations, the UK’s seizure of 61,000 Bitcoin, and OFAC sanctions targeting 146 entities.

Chinese “Cryptoqueen” Qian Zhimin sentenced to 11 years 8 months for money laundering at Southwark Crown Court; UK police seized ~61,000 BTC tied to the case—worth £5B+ at current prices—the largest crypto seizure in UK history. pic.twitter.com/RucbHts2wI

— Crypto Rug Muncher (@CryptoRugMunch) November 12, 2025

🚨On Oct 14, 2025, OFAC, FinCEN, and the UK FCDO take the largest-ever joint sanctions action against cybercriminal networks operating in Southeast Asia.

🔹146 individuals & entities tied to Prince Group TCO sanctioned.
🔹127,271 $BTC (~$15B) seized.
🔹Sever the Huione Group… pic.twitter.com/t1rPWnmhZV

— SlowMist (@SlowMist_Team) October 17, 2025

Chainalysis notes that the transparency of blockchain technology provides unprecedented visibility into these operations, creating unique opportunities for detection and disruption that would be impossible with traditional payment methods.

Who Holds The Key To Accountability?

The pressure is mounting on platforms like Telegram and stablecoin issuers like Tether to take a harder stance.

Telegram claims it removes millions of harmful posts daily, but criminal markets consistently rebuild their channels within hours.

Tether faces mounting criticism for rarely freezing assets tied to trafficking operations, despite having the technical capability to do so immediately.

This regulatory scrutiny will reshape the crypto landscape as lawmakers connect innovation with exploitation.

The industry’s mainstream adoption hinges on addressing these darkest applications of its foundational technologies.

data.title: HTX Ventures and HTX DAO made a multi-dimensional appearance at the Consensus Conference 2026.
data.description:
From February 11th to 12th, the annual Consensus Conference (Consensus 2026), a major global event in the blockchain field, was held in Hong Kong. As leaders in the Web3 industry, HTX Ventures and HTX DAO were deeply involved in the summit. Through their presence at the main venue and numerous high-profile peripheral events, they joined global developers, investment institutions, and industry leaders, showcasing HTX Ventures and HTX DAO's strategic commitment to driving technological innovation and ecosystem prosperity in the industry. HTX Ventures Makes its Debut at Consensus Conference, Justin Sun Delivers Keynote Speech. HTX Ventures made a significant appearance at the Consensus Conference. Against the backdrop of increasingly clear global regulatory frameworks and continuous upgrades to digital asset infrastructure, HTX Ventures focuses on early-stage innovative projects, underlying infrastructure investment, and global ecosystem collaboration. It engages in in-depth exchanges and resource matching with developers, investment institutions, and project teams from around the world, further strengthening its forward-looking layout capabilities in emerging tracks such as AI+Web3, RWA, and modular blockchain.

HTX Ventures Makes a Grand Appearance at the Hong Kong Consensus Conference

About HTX Ventures HTX Ventures is the global investment arm of Huobi HTX, integrating investment, incubation, and research to identify the best and brightest teams globally. As an industry pioneer, HTX Ventures has over 11 years of experience in blockchain development, specializing in identifying cutting-edge technologies and emerging business models in the field. To drive growth within the blockchain ecosystem, we provide comprehensive support to projects, including funding, resources, and strategic advice. HTX Ventures currently supports over 300 projects across multiple blockchain sectors, with some high-quality projects already trading on Huobi HTX. Furthermore, as one of the most active fund of funds (FOFs), HTX Ventures invests in 30 top global funds and partners with leading global blockchain funds such as Polychain, Dragonfly, Bankless, Gitcoin, Figment, Nomad, Animoca, and Hack VC to build the blockchain ecosystem.
